meta.title: DBX-MLP-2: Model Deployment, Serving and A/B Testing

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to log a trained scikit-learn model as an artifact in an active MLflow run so it can later be loaded for inference. Which function call correctly logs the model?

options.text: mlflow.log_artifact(model, 'sklearn-model')
mlflow.log_param('model', model)
mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, artifact_path='sklearn-model')
mlflow.save_model(model, 'sklearn-model')

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because mlflow.sklearn.log_model() serializes a scikit-learn estimator and logs it to the active run with full MLflow model metadata (MLmodel file, conda environment, model signature if provided), enabling later loading via mlflow.pyfunc.load_model().

**A** is wrong because mlflow.log_artifact() logs arbitrary files by path — it expects a file on disk, not a Python model object, and does not produce a proper MLflow model directory with MLmodel metadata.

**B** is wrong because mlflow.log_param() stores key-value string metadata — it cannot serialize or store a model object.

**D** is wrong because mlflow.save_model() saves to a local path but does not log anything to the active run's artifact store.

[MLflow Tracking — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/tracking.html)

questions.stem: Which statement correctly describes the relationship between MLflow experiments and runs?

options.text: A run contains multiple experiments, each with different hyperparameters
Each experiment can only contain one active run at a time
Experiments and runs are the same concept with different names in different MLflow versions
An experiment is a named container that groups multiple runs, where each run records parameters, metrics, and artifacts

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because in MLflow's hierarchy, an experiment is the top-level grouping entity (like a project or model family), and each experiment contains many runs. Each run is one execution of training code that logs its own parameters, metrics, and artifacts.

**A** is wrong because the hierarchy is inverted — experiments contain runs, not the other way around.

**B** is wrong because an experiment can have many concurrent active runs, which is exactly what SparkTrials exploits for parallel hyperparameter search.

**C** is wrong because experiments and runs are distinct concepts at different levels of the hierarchy, not synonyms.

[MLflow Tracking — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/tracking.html)

questions.stem: A data scientist enables mlflow.autolog() before training an XGBoost model. After training completes, they want to additionally log a custom validation metric computed on a held-out test set. What is the correct approach?

options.text: Call mlflow.autolog(disable=True) and then mlflow.log_metric() to replace all autologged metrics
Create a new separate run to log the custom metric so autologged and manual metrics are not mixed
Within the same active run context (or by passing the run_id), call mlflow.log_metric('test_custom_metric', value) after training
Custom metrics cannot be added to a run that used autologging

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because autologging and manual logging are not mutually exclusive. Within the same run context opened by autolog (or by explicitly referencing the run_id using mlflow.start_run(run_id=...)), you can call mlflow.log_metric() to add custom metrics alongside autologged ones.

**A** is wrong because calling mlflow.autolog(disable=True) would disable future autologging but does not allow adding metrics to the existing run — it would end the autolog-started run context.

**B** is wrong because creating a separate run for the custom metric breaks traceability — the custom validation metric and autologged training metrics would be in different runs with no connection.

**D** is wrong because manual logging is always available regardless of autolog status — they coexist within the same run.

[MLflow Tracking — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/tracking.html#automatic-logging)

questions.stem: An ML engineer constructs a HyperOpt search space that includes both a continuous learning rate and a categorical choice of optimizer. Which code correctly defines this combined search space?

options.text: space = {'lr': hp.uniform('lr', 1e-4, 1e-1), 'optimizer': hp.choice('optimizer', ['adam', 'sgd', 'rmsprop'])}
space = {'lr': hp.randint('lr', 100), 'optimizer': hp.uniform('optimizer', 0, 2)}
space = {'lr': hp.choice('lr', [1e-4, 1e-1]), 'optimizer': hp.uniform('optimizer', ['adam', 'sgd'])}
space = [hp.uniform(1e-4, 1e-1), hp.choice(['adam', 'sgd', 'rmsprop'])]

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because hp.uniform() is the appropriate distribution for a continuous float like learning rate, and hp.choice() is correct for a discrete categorical variable like optimizer name. The dict structure with named keys is the standard HyperOpt search space format.

**B** is wrong because hp.randint for a float produces integers, not the continuous range needed for learning rate, and hp.uniform for a categorical optimizer expects numeric bounds, not string lists.

**C** is wrong because hp.choice for learning rate limits it to exactly two discrete values instead of a continuous range, and hp.uniform for optimizer expects numeric bounds rather than a string list.

**D** is wrong because using a list format without named keys means HyperOpt cannot unpack the parameters by name when calling the objective function.

[Hyperopt Best Practices — Databricks Documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/machine-learning/automl-hyperparam-tuning/hyperopt-best-practices.html)

questions.stem: A data scientist uses mlflow.models.infer_signature() to create a model signature. Which inputs are required by infer_signature()?

options.text: A list of feature names and their data types as strings
A sample of the model input data and a sample of the model output (predictions)
The trained model object and the training DataFrame
The MLflow run ID and the artifact path of the logged model

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because `mlflow.models.infer_signature(model_input, model_output)` takes a representative sample of the model input data and a representative sample of the model output (predictions) to infer both the input and output schemas.

**A** is wrong because `infer_signature()` derives schemas from actual data samples, not from manually typed field definitions.

**C** is wrong because `infer_signature()` does not take the trained model object itself; it takes example inputs and outputs.

**D** is wrong because `infer_signature()` operates on example data, not on MLflow run metadata or artifact paths.

[MLflow Model Signatures](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/ml/model/signatures/)

questions.stem: An ML team runs parallel hyperparameter trials using SparkTrials with HyperOpt. Select TWO behaviors that are true of SparkTrials compared to the standard Trials object. (Select TWO)

options.text: SparkTrials distributes each trial as a separate Spark task, allowing multiple trials to run concurrently on cluster workers
When Databricks automated MLflow tracking is enabled and `fmin()` is wrapped in `mlflow.start_run()`, trial runs can be logged to the experiment automatically
SparkTrials enables GPU allocation per trial using the parallelism parameter
SparkTrials requires the objective function to return a dict with 'loss' and 'status' keys, whereas Trials only requires a scalar loss

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because `SparkTrials` dispatches each HyperOpt trial as a Spark task to worker nodes, enabling parallel hyperparameter search across the cluster instead of running trials only on the driver.

**B** is correct because Databricks documents automated MLflow tracking for `SparkTrials`: when you wrap `fmin()` in `mlflow.start_run()`, the experiment can automatically capture the tuning runs for comparison in the MLflow UI.

**C** is wrong because the `parallelism` parameter controls how many trials run concurrently; it does not assign GPUs by itself. GPU usage depends on the cluster and workload configuration.

**D** is wrong because both `Trials` and `SparkTrials` use the same HyperOpt objective-function return pattern, typically a dict containing keys such as `loss` and `status`.

[Parallelize Hyperopt hyperparameter tuning](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/machine-learning/automl-hyperparam-tuning/hyperopt-spark-mlflow-integration)

questions.stem:
A data scientist runs the following code but finds the feature importance figure is NOT visible in the MLflow UI artifacts tab. What is the issue?

```python
with mlflow.start_run():
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    plot_importance(model, ax=ax)
    mlflow.log_artifact('/tmp/importance.png')
```

options.text: plot_importance() does not work with MLflow
mlflow.log_artifact() only works with text files, not PNG images
The figure was never saved to '/tmp/importance.png' before calling log_artifact()
The artifact must be logged before model.fit() completes

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because mlflow.log_artifact() takes a file path and uploads the file at that path to the artifact store. The code never calls fig.savefig('/tmp/importance.png') before log_artifact, so the file does not exist at that path. The fix is to add fig.savefig('/tmp/importance.png') before log_artifact, or use mlflow.log_figure(fig, 'importance.png').

**A** is wrong because plot_importance() from XGBoost works with standard matplotlib figures and has no MLflow incompatibility.

**B** is wrong because mlflow.log_artifact() supports any file type, including images, binary files, and HTML.

**D** is wrong because artifacts can be logged at any point within the run context — before or after training completes.

[MLflow Tracking — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/tracking.html)

questions.stem: A data scientist compares two MLflow runs and notices that Run A was logged with mlflow.xgboost.autolog() while Run B was logged manually. Which metric will appear in Run A's logged metrics that would NOT typically appear in Run B unless explicitly added?

options.text: validation_0-rmse logged at each boosting round (step-wise metric)
Total training wall-clock time
Python interpreter version
Memory usage per tree

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because mlflow.xgboost.autolog() logs evaluation metrics (like validation_0-rmse, validation_0-logloss) at every boosting round as a step-wise time series, which would require manual calls to mlflow.log_metric('rmse', value, step=i) in a custom callback to replicate in Run B.

**B** is wrong because total training wall-clock time can be logged manually and is not a unique feature of XGBoost autolog output.

**C** is wrong because Python interpreter version is captured in the conda environment file artifact, not as a tracked metric.

**D** is wrong because memory usage per tree is not logged by XGBoost autolog — it is not a standard autolog output metric.

[MLflow Tracking — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/tracking.html#automatic-logging)

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to store the path to the training dataset used in an experiment as metadata on the run for reproducibility. Which MLflow function is most appropriate?

options.text: mlflow.log_metric('dataset_path', '/mnt/data/train.parquet')
mlflow.set_tag('dataset_path', '/mnt/data/train.parquet')
mlflow.log_artifact('/mnt/data/train.parquet')
mlflow.log_param('dataset_path', '/mnt/data/train.parquet')

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because mlflow.log_param() is designed for logging configuration values that define the training run setup — including data paths, feature names, preprocessing flags, and hyperparameters. These appear in the run's params table and can be used to filter runs.

**A** is wrong because mlflow.log_metric() only accepts numeric values — passing a string path would fail with a type error.

**B** is wrong because mlflow.set_tag() works technically but tags are for organizational/informational metadata, not reproducibility configuration parameters. The params table is the canonical place for run inputs.

**C** is wrong because mlflow.log_artifact() would copy the entire dataset file into the artifact store, which is expensive and unnecessary when storing just the path reference is sufficient.

[MLflow Tracking — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/tracking.html)

questions.stem: A model is currently in the 'Staging' stage of the MLflow Model Registry. After successful validation, an ML engineer wants to promote it to 'Production'. Using MlflowClient, which method performs this stage transition?

options.text: MlflowClient().update_model_version('MyModel', version=2, description='Promoted')
MlflowClient().transition_model_version_stage('MyModel', version=2, stage='Production')
mlflow.register_model('models:/MyModel/Staging', 'MyModel')
MlflowClient().set_model_version_tag('MyModel', 2, 'stage', 'Production')

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because MlflowClient().transition_model_version_stage() is the dedicated method for moving a model version through the registry lifecycle stages (None → Staging → Production → Archived).

**A** is wrong because MlflowClient().update_model_version() updates the description field of a version but cannot change its stage.

**C** is wrong because mlflow.register_model() from a stage URI would create a new model version rather than transitioning an existing version to a different stage.

**D** is wrong because MlflowClient().set_model_version_tag() sets a metadata tag key-value pair but has no effect on the official stage — the version would still be in Staging.

[MLflow Model Registry — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/model-registry.html)

questions.stem: A data scientist logs a model with a custom PythonModel class that wraps a preprocessing pipeline and a CatBoost classifier. The class implements the predict() method. Which MLflow call correctly logs this custom model?

options.text: mlflow.catboost.log_model(model, 'model')
mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, 'model')
mlflow.pyfunc.log_model('model', python_model=MyCustomModel(), artifacts={'catboost_model': model_path})
mlflow.log_artifact(model, 'model')

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because mlflow.pyfunc.log_model() with a python_model argument is the correct way to log a custom PythonModel subclass. The artifacts dict maps logical names to file paths so the predict() method can access them at inference time via context.artifacts.

**A** is wrong because mlflow.catboost.log_model() requires the mlflow-catboost plugin and only handles native CatBoost models — it does not wrap custom classes with preprocessing pipelines.

**B** is wrong because mlflow.sklearn.log_model() only serializes sklearn-compatible objects using pickle/joblib and does not support custom predict() logic or preprocessing wrappers.

**D** is wrong because mlflow.log_artifact() expects a file path to upload, not a Python class object — passing a class instance would fail with a serialization error.

[MLflow Tracking — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/tracking.html)

questions.stem: An ML team wants to ensure that any client application loading their model always gets the latest 'Production' version without needing to update model version numbers in client code. Which URI pattern achieves this?

options.text: models:/MyModel/3 (hardcoding the current production version number)
runs:/run_id/artifacts/my_model
models:/MyModel/Production (classic registry stage-based URI)
file:///mlflow/models/MyModel

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because the stage-based URI models:/ModelName/Production always resolves to whichever version is currently in the Production stage, making client code immune to version number changes when a new version is promoted.

**A** is wrong because hardcoding the version number means every client application must be updated whenever a new version is promoted to production — a maintenance burden.

**B** is wrong because runs:/run_id/artifacts/my_model is tied to a specific training run artifact, not a promoted registry version with stage semantics.

**D** is wrong because file:///mlflow/models/MyModel is an invalid URI format for the MLflow registry and would not resolve to a registered model.

[MLflow Model Registry — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/model-registry.html)

questions.stem: A Feature Store team is designing feature tables for a recommendation system. Select TWO properties that must be defined when calling FeatureStoreClient.create_table(). (Select TWO)

options.text: name — the full three-part table name (catalog.schema.table)
primary_keys — the column(s) that uniquely identify each entity
partition_by — the column to partition the feature table storage by
schema — the DataFrame schema defining feature column names and types

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the table name (fully qualified as catalog.schema.table) is a required parameter for FeatureStoreClient.create_table().

**B** is correct because primary_keys is required — it defines the entity key(s) used to look up features during training set creation and batch scoring. Without primary_keys, the Feature Store cannot perform joins.

**C** is wrong because partition_by is optional and used for storage optimization, not required for table creation.

**D** is wrong because while schema is needed, it can be inferred from a provided DataFrame via the df parameter or specified via schema — it is not listed among the core required parameters alongside name and primary_keys.

[Databricks Feature Store — Databricks Documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/machine-learning/feature-store/index.html)

questions.stem: A data scientist writes a feature pipeline that calls fs.write_table() with mode='merge'. What is the behavior of 'merge' mode in the Databricks Feature Store?

options.text: Overwrites the entire feature table with the new DataFrame
Appends all rows from the new DataFrame without deduplication
Upserts rows — inserting new entity keys and updating existing entity keys with new feature values
Creates a new version of the feature table without modifying the existing data

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because mode='merge' in FeatureStoreClient.write_table() performs an upsert: rows with primary keys that already exist in the feature table are updated with new values, and rows with new primary keys are inserted.

**A** is wrong because overwriting the entire feature table is the behavior of mode='overwrite', not mode='merge'.

**B** is wrong because appending all rows without deduplication is the behavior of mode='append', which can create duplicate primary keys.

**D** is wrong because feature tables do not have a versioning system in the write_table() interface — there is no mode that creates a new version without modifying existing data.

[Databricks Feature Store — Databricks Documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/machine-learning/feature-store/index.html)

questions.stem: An ML engineer needs to find all runs in an experiment where val_f1 > 0.85 and the regularization parameter alpha is less than 0.01. Using MlflowClient, which filter_string should they use?

options.text: "metrics.val_f1 > 0.85 OR params.alpha < 0.01"
"metrics.val_f1 > 0.85 AND params.alpha < '0.01'"
"val_f1 > 0.85 AND alpha < 0.01"
"metrics.val_f1 > 0.85 AND params.alpha < 0.01"

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because MLflow search filter strings require the namespace prefix — metrics. for metric values and params. for parameter values — and AND combines conditions correctly.

**A** is wrong because it uses OR instead of AND, which would match runs satisfying either condition, not both.

**B** is wrong because wrapping the param value in quotes ('0.01') makes it a string comparison, which breaks numeric ordering for params stored as strings in MLflow.

**C** is wrong because it omits the required metrics. and params. namespace prefixes — bare field names like val_f1 and alpha are not valid filter expressions in MLflow search syntax.

[MLflow Tracking — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/tracking.html)

questions.stem: An ML engineer wants to add a human-readable description to a registered model version explaining the changes made. Which MlflowClient call accomplishes this?

options.text: MlflowClient().set_model_version_tag('MyModel', 2, 'description', 'Improved recall')
mlflow.set_tag('model_description', 'Improved recall with new training data')
MlflowClient().transition_model_version_stage('MyModel', 2, stage='Production', archive_existing_versions=True)
MlflowClient().update_model_version('MyModel', version=2, description='Improved recall with new training data')

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because MlflowClient().update_model_version() is the dedicated method for updating the description field on a specific model version in the registry. The description appears in the Model Registry UI.

**A** is wrong because MlflowClient().set_model_version_tag() adds a tag key-value pair to the version's metadata, which is searchable but is not the same as the official description field.

**B** is wrong because mlflow.set_tag() sets a tag on the active training run, not on a registered model version in the registry.

**C** is wrong because MlflowClient().transition_model_version_stage() changes the stage of a model version, not its description.

[MLflow Model Registry — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/model-registry.html)

questions.stem: A data scientist logs a model with mlflow.sklearn.log_model() but then discovers the model was logged without a model signature. Without retraining, how can they add a signature to the existing registered model version?

options.text: Call mlflow.register_model() again with a signature argument to overwrite the version
Download the model artifacts, add the signature using mlflow.models.set_signature(), then re-upload the artifacts
Signatures can only be added at training time — the model must be retrained
Set a tag on the model version with key 'signature' containing the JSON schema

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because MLflow provides mlflow.models.set_signature() which modifies the signature of an existing logged model in-place in the artifact store, without requiring retraining.

**A** is wrong because mlflow.register_model() would create a new model version rather than updating the signature of the existing version.

**C** is wrong because mlflow.models.set_signature() exists precisely for this post-hoc scenario — signatures can be added without retraining.

**D** is wrong because tags are metadata stored externally and are not read by MLflow serving infrastructure as a model signature.

[mlflow.sklearn — MLflow Python API](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/python_api/mlflow.sklearn.html)

questions.stem: A Feature Store table stores daily aggregated user behavior features. An ML team wants to create a training set that joins these features to label events using point-in-time lookups. What additional configuration is required in the FeatureLookup definition?

options.text: Set output_name to specify where to write the joined features
Set mode='point_in_time' in create_training_set()
Provide a join_type='left' argument to handle missing feature values
Specify timestamp_lookup_key in the FeatureLookup to indicate which column in the labels DataFrame holds the event timestamp

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because to enable point-in-time lookups when calling create_training_set(), each FeatureLookup must specify a timestamp_lookup_key — the column in the observation/labels DataFrame that holds the event timestamp.

**A** is wrong because output_name renames the feature column in the output DataFrame, which has nothing to do with enabling point-in-time correctness.

**B** is wrong because mode='point_in_time' is not a valid parameter for create_training_set() — point-in-time behavior is controlled via timestamp_lookup_key in FeatureLookup.

**C** is wrong because join_type is not a parameter of FeatureLookup — the join behavior is determined by the feature table's timestamp key configuration.

[Databricks Feature Store — Databricks Documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/machine-learning/feature-store/index.html)

questions.stem: A data scientist creates a Spark UDF from an MLflow model for batch inference. What is the main difference between using env_manager='conda' versus env_manager='local' in mlflow.pyfunc.spark_udf()?

options.text: env_manager='conda' creates a conda environment matching the model's dependencies on each worker, ensuring reproducibility; env_manager='local' uses the existing Python environment on workers
env_manager='conda' runs inference on the driver only; env_manager='local' uses all workers
env_manager='conda' only works with sklearn models; env_manager='local' works with all flavors
env_manager='conda' is required for GPU models; env_manager='local' is for CPU models

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because env_manager='conda' instructs each worker to create a conda environment from the model's conda.yaml, ensuring the exact library versions used during training are present at inference time. env_manager='local' uses whatever Python environment is already installed on the workers, which is faster to start but risks version mismatches.

**B** is wrong — both options distribute inference across workers.

**C** is wrong — both work with all model flavors.

**D** is wrong — GPU/CPU configuration is unrelated to the env_manager choice.

[mlflow.pyfunc — MLflow Python API](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/python_api/mlflow.pyfunc.html)

questions.stem: An ML team wants to serve multiple model versions simultaneously on a Databricks Model Serving endpoint. They configure the endpoint to send 80% of traffic to version 3 and 20% to version 4. After two weeks, version 4 shows higher accuracy. What is the next step in this deployment strategy?

options.text: Delete version 3 from the Model Registry and redeploy the endpoint with only version 4
Retrain version 3 with version 4's hyperparameters to make them equivalent
Create a new separate endpoint for version 4 and update DNS routing
Update the endpoint's traffic split to 100% version 4 and 0% version 3, then monitor before retiring version 3

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because the standard A/B rollout completion pattern is to gradually shift traffic to the winning version — setting version 4 to 100% and version 3 to 0% within the same endpoint — while keeping version 3 registered and reachable for quick rollback.

**A** is wrong because deleting version 3 from the Model Registry eliminates the rollback option if issues emerge after full promotion.

**B** is wrong because retraining version 3 with version 4's hyperparameters is counterproductive — if version 4 is better, the goal is to promote version 4, not equalize them.

**C** is wrong because creating a new separate endpoint for version 4 requires clients to update their DNS/URL and adds infrastructure complexity with no benefit over adjusting the existing endpoint's traffic split.

[MLflow Model Registry — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/model-registry.html)

questions.stem: An ML team wants to score a batch of 100K new customer records using a model that was trained using the Databricks Feature Store, where features are stored in a table with 'customer_id' as the primary key. Which FeatureStoreClient call is correct for batch scoring?

options.text: fs.score_batch('models:/ChurnModel/Production', df_with_features)
fs.predict('models:/ChurnModel/Production', df_with_customer_ids)
fs.score_batch('models:/ChurnModel/Production', df_with_customer_ids)
mlflow.pyfunc.load_model('models:/ChurnModel/Production').predict(df_with_customer_ids)

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because FeatureStoreClient.score_batch() only requires a DataFrame containing the primary key column(s) (customer_id) plus any request-time columns. It automatically retrieves the required features from the Feature Store using the model's logged feature lineage metadata.

**A** is wrong because if df_with_features already contains all features, you bypass the Feature Store's automatic lookup — and score_batch() is specifically designed to accept just entity keys, not a pre-joined feature DataFrame.

**B** is wrong because fs.predict() is not a valid FeatureStoreClient method.

**D** is wrong because loading the model with mlflow.pyfunc.load_model() bypasses Feature Store metadata, so automatic feature lookup is unavailable — the input DataFrame would need to already contain all features manually joined.

[mlflow.pyfunc — MLflow Python API](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/python_api/mlflow.pyfunc.html)

questions.stem: A team is evaluating real-time model serving latency on Databricks. Select TWO factors that most directly increase inference latency on a Model Serving endpoint. (Select TWO)

options.text: Cold start time when the endpoint scales from zero to one instance after a period of inactivity
The number of model versions registered in the MLflow Model Registry
Heavy preprocessing or feature transformation logic executed inside the model's predict() method
The number of unused experiments in the MLflow tracking server

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because when an endpoint scales to zero and receives a new request, it must spin up a container, load the model, and initialize the environment — adding seconds of cold start latency to that first request.

**C** is correct because any preprocessing (e.g., tokenization, complex feature engineering, large dataframe transforms) inside the predict() method executes serially on every request, directly adding to p99 latency.

**B** is wrong because the number of registry versions does not affect serving endpoint latency — the endpoint only loads the served version.

**D** is wrong because unused experiments in the tracking server have no impact on the serving endpoint.

[MLflow Model Registry — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/model-registry.html)

questions.stem: An ML engineer deploys a Structured Streaming pipeline that uses foreachBatch to apply a fraud detection model to micro-batches. The model is loaded inside the batch function. After running for 24 hours, they notice executor memory pressure. What is the root cause and the fix?

options.text: The model grows in size over time; fix by periodically calling gc.collect() inside the batch function
Streaming checkpoints are storing model weights; fix by disabling checkpointing
foreachBatch does not support model inference; migrate to a DataFrame API approach
The model is reloaded into worker memory on every micro-batch, accumulating without being released; fix by loading the model once outside the streaming query and broadcasting it

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because when mlflow.pyfunc.load_model() is called inside the foreachBatch function, a new copy of the model is loaded into executor JVM/Python worker memory on every micro-batch invocation. If micro-batches arrive faster than the garbage collector can reclaim the old model objects, memory accumulates, causing pressure. The fix is to load the model once before the streaming query starts and broadcast it using SparkContext.broadcast(), so each executor gets one copy that persists across batches.

**A** is wrong — model weights don't grow dynamically.

**C** is wrong — foreachBatch fully supports model inference.

**B** is wrong — checkpointing stores query state metadata, not model weights.

[mlflow.pyfunc — MLflow Python API](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/python_api/mlflow.pyfunc.html)

questions.stem: A batch inference job uses mlflow.pyfunc.spark_udf() and runs on a 10-node cluster. The job completes but 3 worker nodes have error logs showing 'ModuleNotFoundError: No module named lightgbm'. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The LightGBM model was not logged correctly in MLflow
The model's MLflow flavor is incompatible with the Spark version
The spark_udf() was called with env_manager='local' and lightgbm is not installed on those 3 worker nodes
The result_type parameter was set incorrectly for a LightGBM model

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because env_manager='local' uses the Python environment already installed on each worker. If lightgbm was not installed uniformly on all cluster nodes — a common situation with autoscaled clusters where new nodes may not have all packages — some workers will fail with ModuleNotFoundError.

**A** is wrong because a logging issue with the model would cause a different error at model load time, not a ModuleNotFoundError at inference time on specific workers.

**B** is wrong because LightGBM and Spark version compatibility is a separate concern and would cause a different type of error, not a missing module error on only some workers.

**D** is wrong because an incorrect result_type causes type errors or shape mismatches in the UDF output, not a ModuleNotFoundError.

[mlflow.pyfunc — MLflow Python API](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/python_api/mlflow.pyfunc.html)

questions.stem: An ML engineer converts a scikit-learn model to ONNX for edge deployment. After conversion, they observe that the ONNX model's predictions for some inputs differ slightly from the original sklearn model. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: ONNX conversion applies additional L2 regularization to the model weights
The ONNX Runtime version must exactly match the sklearn version to produce identical results
ONNX does not support the model architecture and is silently approximating it
Floating-point precision differences between sklearn's Python/numpy operations and ONNX Runtime's optimized operations

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because ONNX Runtime executes computation graphs using highly optimized kernels with different floating-point operation ordering than numpy, which introduces small numerical differences due to floating-point non-associativity. These are typically in the 1e-6 to 1e-4 range and are expected.

**A** is wrong because ONNX conversion does not modify model weights or add regularization — it is a functional conversion of the computation graph.

**B** is wrong because there is no requirement for version matching between sklearn and ONNX Runtime — the ONNX format is an interoperability standard.

**C** is wrong because sklearn-onnx (skl2onnx) fully supports standard sklearn estimators and performs exact functional conversion — it does not silently approximate.

[Deploy Models with Model Serving — Databricks Documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/machine-learning/model-serving/index.html)

questions.stem:
A model is served via a Databricks Model Serving REST endpoint. A developer sends a prediction request using the following curl command:

```bash
curl -X POST https://<workspace>/serving-endpoints/MyModel/invocations \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"dataframe_records": [{"feature1": 1.5, "feature2": 0.3}]}'
```

The endpoint returns a 400 error. The model was logged with a signature requiring feature1 and feature2 as floats. What is the most likely issue?

options.text: The JSON payload format should use 'inputs' instead of 'dataframe_records'
The Authorization header format is incorrect for Databricks endpoints
The feature1 value 1.5 exceeds the maximum allowed float value
The endpoint name 'MyModel' cannot contain uppercase letters

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Databricks Model Serving (backed by MLflow serving) supports two payload formats: 'dataframe_split' (with columns and data keys) and 'dataframe_records' (list of dicts). However, some MLflow model server versions expect 'inputs' as the outer key for tensor-based inputs. If the endpoint is configured for the 'inputs' format (common for non-pyfunc models or specific signature types), 'dataframe_records' would return a 400. The correct format for pyfunc models is typically {'dataframe_records': [...]} or {'dataframe_split': {'columns': [...], 'data': [...]}}.

**B** is wrong because 'Bearer <token>' is the correct Databricks auth format.

**C** is wrong because there is no maximum float cap.

**D** is wrong because endpoint names can contain uppercase letters.

questions.stem: An ML team wants to detect drift in a categorical feature (e.g., 'product_category' with 15 possible values). Which statistical test is most appropriate?

options.text: Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) test
Chi-squared goodness-of-fit test
T-test for independent samples
Mann-Whitney U test

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the chi-squared goodness-of-fit test compares the observed frequency distribution of categorical values in production against the expected distribution from training, making it the standard choice for categorical drift detection.

**A** is wrong because the Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) test measures differences between two continuous cumulative distribution functions — it is not appropriate for categorical data with discrete counts.

**C** is wrong because the t-test for independent samples compares the means of two continuous numeric distributions — it cannot be applied to categorical frequency counts.

**D** is wrong because the Mann-Whitney U test is a non-parametric test comparing ranks of continuous distributions between two groups, not categorical frequency distributions.

[Machine Learning on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/machine-learning/index.html)

questions.stem: A production ML model's Population Stability Index (PSI) for a key feature is calculated as 0.28. How should the monitoring team interpret this value and what action should they take?

options.text: PSI < 0.1 means no shift; 0.1-0.25 means moderate shift; PSI > 0.25 means significant shift requiring investigation. PSI=0.28 signals significant shift — trigger model review and potential retraining.
PSI is on a 0-1 scale; 0.28 means 28% of the feature distribution has shifted, which is within the acceptable 30% threshold.
PSI > 0 always requires immediate model retraining regardless of magnitude.
PSI of 0.28 indicates 72% model accuracy on the shifted distribution.

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the standard PSI interpretation thresholds are: PSI < 0.1 (no significant shift), 0.1-0.25 (moderate shift, monitor closely), PSI > 0.25 (significant shift, investigate and consider retraining). A PSI of 0.28 exceeds the 0.25 threshold, signaling significant distribution shift that warrants model review.

**B** is wrong because PSI is not a percentage scale — it has no upper bound and values above 0.25 indicate significant shift regardless of whether 0.30 is also significant.

**C** is wrong because minor PSI values (< 0.1) do not require retraining.

**D** is wrong — PSI has no relationship to accuracy percentage.

questions.stem: A Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring job is configured on an inference table that stores model predictions and ground truth labels. What type of monitor should the team configure to track model quality metrics (precision, recall) over time?

options.text: TimeSeries monitor
InferenceLog monitor
Snapshot monitor
Profile monitor

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring's InferenceLog monitor type is specifically designed for tables that contain model predictions alongside ground truth labels. It computes model quality metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, RMSE) over time windows.

**A** is wrong because a TimeSeries monitor is for tables with a time dimension tracking non-ML time-series data — it does not have built-in model quality metric computation.

**C** is wrong because a Snapshot monitor computes statistics for a single point-in-time profile of a table rather than tracking model quality over rolling time windows.

**D** is wrong because a Profile monitor generates statistical summaries of a table's columns but is not the specific type for inference log analysis with ground truth labels.

[Machine Learning on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/machine-learning/index.html)

questions.stem: An ML team is designing an automated retraining pipeline that triggers when model performance degrades in production. Select TWO signals that would be appropriate triggers for automated retraining. (Select TWO)

options.text: The 7-day rolling F1 score on labeled production data drops below 0.80, compared to a baseline of 0.87 at deployment
The number of prediction requests to the serving endpoint exceeds 10,000 per hour
The KS test p-value for a key numerical feature drops below 0.05 for three consecutive monitoring windows
A new version of the ML framework (e.g., scikit-learn) is released

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a statistically significant and sustained drop in F1 score against ground truth labels is a direct indicator of model performance degradation — the clearest trigger for retraining.

**B** is wrong because high request volume indicates traffic patterns and a scaling concern, not model degradation — increased load does not mean predictions have become less accurate.

**C** is correct because sustained input feature drift (KS p-value < 0.05) for three consecutive windows suggests the data generating process has changed enough that the current model is likely making suboptimal predictions on the new distribution.

**D** is wrong because a new framework release is a dependency management concern and has no bearing on whether the current deployed model's predictions have degraded.

questions.stem: A data scientist calls mlflow.active_run() inside a training function. Under which condition does this return None?

options.text: When called before the first training run has ever been started in the session
When mlflow.autolog() is enabled — autolog disables the active run context
When the MLflow tracking server is not reachable
When the function is called on a worker node rather than the driver

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because mlflow.active_run() returns the currently active Run object if a run is open (via mlflow.start_run() or autolog), and returns None when no run is currently active — such as when called outside any run context or before any run has been started.

**B** is wrong — autolog opens a run automatically, so active_run() would return that run.

**C** is wrong — network connectivity affects logging calls but active_run() reads local Python state, not the server.

**D** is wrong — active_run() operates on driver-side Python state; it is not typically called inside Spark worker functions.

questions.stem: A data scientist uses mlflow.evaluate() with a custom metric function. They define `def my_metric(eval_df, builtin_metrics): return MetricValue(aggregate_results={'my_score': 0.92})`. Which parameter of mlflow.evaluate() accepts this function?

options.text: extra_metrics=[make_metric(eval_fn=my_metric, greater_is_better=True)]
custom_metrics=[my_metric]
metric_functions={'my_metric': my_metric}
evaluators=[my_metric]

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because mlflow.evaluate() accepts custom metric functions through the extra_metrics parameter, which takes a list of EvaluationMetric objects created with mlflow.models.make_metric(). make_metric() wraps the custom function and requires specifying greater_is_better to determine optimality direction.

**B** is wrong because custom_metrics is not the correct parameter name for mlflow.evaluate().

**C** is wrong because no dict-based metric_functions parameter exists.

**D** is wrong because evaluators specifies which evaluator backend to use (e.g., 'default', 'shap'), not custom metric functions.

[MLflow Tracking — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/tracking.html)

questions.stem: An ML team wants to share an MLflow experiment between two Databricks workspaces in the same account. A data scientist in workspace B needs read access to runs created in workspace A. What is the correct approach in Databricks?

options.text: Export the experiment as a JSON file from workspace A and import it into workspace B
Change the tracking URI in workspace B to point to workspace A's tracking server
Duplicate the runs by re-logging all metrics and artifacts in workspace B
Move all experiments to a Unity Catalog-backed MLflow tracking server accessible to both workspaces

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because Databricks Unity Catalog provides a unified governance layer across workspaces. When MLflow experiments are stored in a Unity Catalog-enabled shared metastore, users in both workspaces can access the same experiments and runs with appropriate permissions.

**A** is wrong because exporting as JSON and importing is a one-time migration technique, not a live sharing mechanism — changes after the export are not visible in workspace B.

**B** is wrong because changing the tracking URI to point to workspace A's server is not a supported cross-workspace access pattern — each workspace's MLflow server is scoped to that workspace unless using Unity Catalog.

**C** is wrong because duplicating runs by re-logging data creates synchronization problems and doubles storage costs without providing a live shared view.

[MLflow Tracking — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/tracking.html)

questions.stem: An ML engineer runs nested runs for an ensemble model: a parent run for the ensemble and child runs for each base learner. They call mlflow.start_run(nested=True) for each child. After all child runs finish, where should the ensemble model artifact be logged?

options.text: To one of the child runs that performed best, so the best base learner is promoted
To the parent run, since the ensemble represents the combined outcome of all base learners
To a separate new run created after the nested runs complete
To all child runs simultaneously using mlflow.log_artifact() with a shared artifact path

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because in the nested run pattern for ensembles, the parent run represents the combined outcome of all base learners. The final ensemble model should be logged to the parent run so the artifact URI and model registration are associated with the ensemble entity.

**A** is wrong because logging the ensemble to a child run conflates the component (one base learner) with the aggregate result (the ensemble) — models loaded from a child run URI would only be the base learner, not the ensemble.

**C** is wrong because creating a separate new run after the nested runs complete fragments the experiment — the ensemble has no connection to the base learner runs in the MLflow UI.

**D** is technically possible but violates the intent of the nesting structure and is not the standard pattern — the ensemble is the parent-level artifact.

[MLflow Tracking — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/tracking.html)

questions.stem: A data scientist defines a HyperOpt search space with hp.quniform('batch_size', 32, 512, 32). What values can this expression produce?

options.text: Any integer between 32 and 512, uniformly sampled
A float uniformly sampled from (32, 512) then quantized to the nearest integer
Exactly 32 or 512 with equal probability
Multiples of 32 between 32 and 512: [32, 64, 96, ..., 512]

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because hp.quniform(label, low, high, q) samples from round(uniform(low, high) / q) * q, producing values that are multiples of q within the [low, high] range. With q=32, the possible values are 32, 64, 96, ..., 512 — exactly the common batch size choices that are powers of 2 or multiples of 32, which is a practical hyperparameter constraint.

**A** is wrong — quniform is quantized, not arbitrary integers.

**C** is wrong — only the endpoints are not guaranteed; all multiples of q are sampled with roughly equal probability.

**B** is wrong — the output is quantized to multiples of q, not to the nearest integer.

[Hyperopt Best Practices — Databricks Documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/machine-learning/automl-hyperparam-tuning/hyperopt-best-practices.html)

questions.stem: A data scientist calls mlflow.log_artifact('/tmp/model_card.html', artifact_path='documentation') and mlflow.log_artifacts('/tmp/plots/', artifact_path='visualizations') within the same run. Select TWO true statements about where these files are stored in the artifact store. (Select TWO)

options.text: model_card.html will be stored at 'documentation/model_card.html' relative to the run's artifact root
All files inside /tmp/plots/ will be stored at 'visualizations/<filename>' relative to the run's artifact root
log_artifacts() with artifact_path will ignore subdirectories inside /tmp/plots/
Both calls write to the same artifact root directory, overwriting each other if filenames clash

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because mlflow.log_artifact(local_path, artifact_path) stores the single file at '<artifact_path>/<filename>' — so model_card.html lands at 'documentation/model_card.html' in the artifact store.

**B** is correct because mlflow.log_artifacts(local_dir, artifact_path) recursively uploads all files in the local directory, placing each at '<artifact_path>/<relative_file_path>' — so every file in /tmp/plots/ lands under 'visualizations/'.

**C** is wrong — log_artifacts() does preserve directory structure for subdirectories.

**D** is wrong — the artifact_path parameters 'documentation' and 'visualizations' are different namespaces, so they cannot overwrite each other.

[MLflow Tracking — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/tracking.html)

questions.stem: A data scientist wants the default artifact storage location for new MLflow experiments to be a specific DBFS path. Which MLflow function sets this at experiment creation time?

options.text: mlflow.set_tracking_uri('dbfs:/mlflow/artifacts')
mlflow.create_experiment('MyExp', artifact_location='dbfs:/mlflow/artifacts/MyExp')
mlflow.set_experiment('MyExp', artifact_location='dbfs:/mlflow/artifacts/MyExp')
mlflow.log_artifact('/dev/null', artifact_path='dbfs:/mlflow/artifacts/MyExp')

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because mlflow.create_experiment() accepts an optional artifact_location parameter that sets the default root URI where all run artifacts for that experiment are stored. This must be set at creation time and cannot be changed afterward.

**A** is wrong because mlflow.set_tracking_uri() sets where the MLflow tracking server is located (the backend store URL), not where artifacts are stored within an experiment.

**C** is wrong because mlflow.set_experiment() sets the active experiment context by name but does not accept an artifact_location parameter.

**D** is wrong because mlflow.log_artifact() is for uploading a specific file to the current run's artifact store — it is not a way to configure the experiment's default storage location.

[MLflow Tracking — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/tracking.html)

questions.stem: A data scientist uses mlflow.log_input() to log a training dataset with source='dbfs:/data/train.delta'. Later, an auditor queries the dataset inputs for model version 7 of 'RiskModel'. Which sequence of calls retrieves this information?

options.text: client.get_registered_model('RiskModel').dataset_inputs
client.get_model_version('RiskModel', '7').dataset_inputs
version = client.get_model_version('RiskModel', '7'); run = client.get_run(version.run_id); run.inputs.dataset_inputs
mlflow.search_runs(filter_string="tags.dataset_source='dbfs:/data/train.delta'")

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because dataset inputs logged with mlflow.log_input() are attached to the training run as RunInputs. The retrieval sequence is: (1) get the model version to find its run_id, (2) get the run using that run_id, (3) access run.inputs.dataset_inputs.

**A** is wrong because registered model objects (from client.get_registered_model()) do not have a dataset_inputs attribute — that information is on the run, not the model.

**B** is wrong because model version objects (from client.get_model_version()) also do not have a dataset_inputs attribute directly — you must navigate to the associated run.

**D** is wrong because mlflow.search_runs() with a tag filter would find runs but does not return structured DatasetInput objects — it also assumes the dataset was stored as a tag, not a logged input.

[MLflow Tracking — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/tracking.html)

questions.stem: An ML engineer wants to log both training and validation metrics to a single MLflow run using a single function call. They have a dict {'train_loss': 0.12, 'val_loss': 0.18, 'val_acc': 0.94}. Which call logs all three at once?

options.text: mlflow.log_metric('train_loss', 0.12); mlflow.log_metric('val_loss', 0.18); mlflow.log_metric('val_acc', 0.94)
mlflow.set_tags({'train_loss': 0.12, 'val_loss': 0.18, 'val_acc': 0.94})
mlflow.log_params({'train_loss': 0.12, 'val_loss': 0.18, 'val_acc': 0.94})
mlflow.log_metrics({'train_loss': 0.12, 'val_loss': 0.18, 'val_acc': 0.94})

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because mlflow.log_metrics() (plural) accepts a dict of metric_name → float_value pairs and logs all of them in a single API call, which is more efficient than individual calls.

**A** is wrong because making three separate mlflow.log_metric() calls works but is inefficient — each call is a separate HTTP request to the tracking server, adding unnecessary overhead.

**B** is wrong because mlflow.set_tags() stores values as string tags, not numeric metrics — they cannot be compared numerically or plotted on the metrics chart.

**C** is wrong because mlflow.log_params() stores key-value pairs as hyperparameter strings in the params table — they would appear in the params panel, not in the metrics chart, and cannot be plotted over time.

[MLflow Tracking — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/tracking.html)

questions.stem: An MLOps engineer needs to set up an automated notification to a Slack channel whenever a model version transitions to the Staging stage in the MLflow Model Registry. Which MLflow registry webhook event should they use?

options.text: MODEL_VERSION_CREATED
TRANSITION_REQUEST_CREATED
MODEL_VERSION_TRANSITIONED_STAGE
REGISTERED_MODEL_CREATED

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because MODEL_VERSION_TRANSITIONED_STAGE fires when a model version's stage is actually changed (e.g., to Staging or Production). This is the correct event for triggering post-transition notifications.

**A** is wrong because MODEL_VERSION_CREATED fires when a new version is first registered, before any stage assignment — it does not indicate a stage transition.

**B** is wrong because TRANSITION_REQUEST_CREATED fires when a transition is requested but not yet approved — useful for approval workflows, not post-transition Slack notifications.

**D** is wrong because REGISTERED_MODEL_CREATED fires when a new model name is first registered in the registry, not when an existing version transitions stages.

[MLflow Model Registry — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/model-registry.html)

questions.stem: A data scientist logs a model with mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, 'model', extra_pip_requirements=['shap==0.42.0', 'pandas>=1.3']). How are these extra requirements stored in the model artifact?

options.text: They replace the auto-generated conda.yaml entirely with only the specified packages
They are appended to the model's requirements.txt file, which is used alongside conda.yaml at serving time
They are stored as a run tag with key 'extra_pip_requirements'
They override the conda.yaml by replacing the pip section with only the extra requirements

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because extra_pip_requirements are appended to the model's requirements.txt file (not the conda.yaml). The requirements.txt is generated alongside conda.yaml in the model artifact directory. MLflow serving environments install both conda.yaml dependencies and requirements.txt dependencies. The extra_pip_requirements are additive — they do not replace the auto-detected dependencies.

**A** is wrong because the conda.yaml is auto-generated with all detected packages plus extra ones.

**C** is wrong — requirements are stored in the model artifact, not run tags.

**D** is wrong — extra_pip_requirements are appended to requirements.txt, not used to override the conda.yaml pip section.

[mlflow.sklearn — MLflow Python API](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/python_api/mlflow.sklearn.html)

questions.stem: A Unity Catalog model 'prod.ml.ChurnPredictor' has version 5 tagged as 'champion'. A new version 6 is trained and passes validation. The team wants to atomically promote version 6 to champion and demote version 5 to challenger without a gap. Which set of calls achieves this?

options.text: client.delete_registered_model_alias('prod.ml.ChurnPredictor', 'champion'); client.set_registered_model_alias('prod.ml.ChurnPredictor', 'champion', 6)
client.set_registered_model_alias('prod.ml.ChurnPredictor', 'champion', 6); client.set_registered_model_alias('prod.ml.ChurnPredictor', 'challenger', 5)
client.transition_model_version_stage('prod.ml.ChurnPredictor', 5, 'Archived'); client.transition_model_version_stage('prod.ml.ChurnPredictor', 6, 'Production')
client.update_model_version('prod.ml.ChurnPredictor', version=6, alias='champion')

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because in the Unity Catalog registry, set_registered_model_alias() reassigns an alias to a new version. When you call set_registered_model_alias('...', 'champion', 6), the alias 'champion' is atomically moved from version 5 to version 6 — there is no gap because the alias always points to exactly one version. The second call assigns the 'challenger' alias to version 5.

**A** is wrong because deleting the alias first creates a gap where no champion exists.

**C** is wrong because transition_model_version_stage() is a classic registry method, not available in Unity Catalog (which uses aliases instead of stages).

**D** is wrong because update_model_version() has no alias parameter.

[MLflow Model Registry — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/model-registry.html)

questions.stem: A data science team is evaluating using Databricks Feature Store vs. manual feature engineering for a production ML pipeline. Select TWO advantages of using the Feature Store's log_model() and score_batch() workflow over manual Spark joins + spark_udf(). (Select TWO)

options.text: The model artifact records which feature tables it depends on, enabling automatic feature retrieval at inference time from just entity keys
Feature Store log_model() automatically performs hyperparameter tuning before serializing the model
Eliminates the need to write and maintain join logic between entity tables and feature tables in the inference pipeline
Feature Store models are automatically deployed to a Model Serving endpoint upon registration

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because FeatureStoreClient.log_model() records the feature lineage (which tables, which keys, which columns) as model metadata. At inference time, score_batch() uses this metadata to automatically fetch the correct features — the caller only provides entity primary keys.

**C** is correct because this lineage-driven auto-lookup eliminates manual join code in inference pipelines, reducing maintenance burden and preventing join logic drift between training and serving.

**B** is wrong — Feature Store has no hyperparameter optimization capability.

**D** is wrong — Feature Store model registration does not automatically create a serving endpoint; that is a separate Databricks Model Serving action.

[Databricks Feature Store — Databricks Documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/machine-learning/feature-store/index.html)

questions.stem: An ML engineer wants to search for all registered model versions of 'SalesModel' that are currently in the 'Production' stage. Which filter_string correctly retrieves them?

options.text: MlflowClient().search_model_versions("name='SalesModel' AND stage='Production'")
MlflowClient().get_latest_versions('SalesModel', stages=['Production'])
MlflowClient().search_model_versions("name='SalesModel'") and then filter client-side by version.current_stage == 'Production'
MlflowClient().list_model_versions('SalesModel', stage='Production')

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because search_model_versions() supports filtering by 'stage' as a filter attribute in the filter string, allowing server-side filtering. This is the most efficient approach for retrieving all Production versions of a specific model.

**B** is wrong because get_latest_versions() returns only the single latest version per requested stage — if multiple versions are in Production simultaneously, earlier ones would be missed.

**C** is wrong because retrieving all versions and filtering client-side works but is inefficient for large model registries — it fetches all versions across all stages before discarding non-Production ones.

**D** is wrong because list_model_versions() with a stage parameter does not exist as a valid MlflowClient method.

[MLflow Model Registry — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/model-registry.html)

questions.stem: A data scientist needs to programmatically retrieve the artifact URI of a specific model version so they can inspect the raw model files. Given version = client.get_model_version('MyModel', '3'), which attribute contains the artifact URI?

options.text: version.artifact_uri
version.source
version.run_artifact_uri
version.model_uri

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the ModelVersion object returned by get_model_version() stores the artifact location in the 'source' attribute — this is the runs:/<run_id>/<artifact_path> URI pointing to the original logged model in the run's artifact store.

**A** is wrong because version.artifact_uri is not a standard attribute on the ModelVersion object — this field does not exist on the returned protobuf object.

**C** is wrong because version.run_artifact_uri does not exist as an attribute on ModelVersion.

**D** is wrong because version.model_uri is not a ModelVersion attribute — the models:/ URI is constructed from the model name and version number by the caller, not stored as a field on the object.

[MLflow Model Registry — MLflow Documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/model-registry.html)

questions.stem: An ML team uses Databricks Feature Store to build training datasets. They have a label DataFrame with columns ['user_id', 'churn_label', 'event_ts'] and a feature table 'catalog.features.user_activity' with primary key 'user_id' and timestamp key 'activity_ts'. Which FeatureLookup correctly performs point-in-time lookup using event_ts from the label DataFrame?

options.text: FeatureLookup(table_name='catalog.features.user_activity', feature_names=['clicks_7d', 'sessions_30d'], lookup_key='user_id')
FeatureLookup(table_name='catalog.features.user_activity', lookup_key='user_id', point_in_time=True)
FeatureLookup(table_name='catalog.features.user_activity', feature_names=['clicks_7d', 'sessions_30d'], lookup_key='user_id', timestamp_lookup_key='activity_ts')
FeatureLookup(table_name='catalog.features.user_activity', feature_names=['clicks_7d', 'sessions_30d'], lookup_key='user_id', timestamp_lookup_key='event_ts')

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because timestamp_lookup_key must reference the column in the label/observation DataFrame (not the feature table) that holds the event timestamp. In this case, 'event_ts' is the observation time in the label DataFrame, and the Feature Store will look up feature values from 'user_activity' as of each row's 'event_ts'.

**A** is wrong because it omits timestamp_lookup_key, disabling point-in-time correctness.

**C** is wrong because 'activity_ts' is the feature table's own timestamp column, not the label DataFrame's event timestamp — timestamp_lookup_key must reference the observation time in the label data.

**B** is wrong because point_in_time is not a valid FeatureLookup parameter.

[Databricks Feature Store — Databricks Documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/machine-learning/feature-store/index.html)

questions.stem: After registering a model, an ML engineer uses mlflow.artifacts.download_artifacts(run_id='abc123', artifact_path='model') to retrieve the model files. What is the local directory structure that results from this call?

options.text: A single file named 'model' is downloaded to the current working directory
The full artifact directory at 'model' path in run abc123 is downloaded, including MLmodel file, conda.yaml, python_env.yaml, and the model weights
Only the MLmodel manifest file is downloaded; model weights require a separate call
The download fails because artifact_path must be a full URI, not a relative path

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because mlflow.artifacts.download_artifacts() with a run_id and artifact_path downloads the entire artifact subtree at that path. MLflow model artifacts are directories containing: the MLmodel manifest, conda.yaml, python_env.yaml, requirements.txt, and the serialized model weights (e.g., model.pkl for sklearn, model.pt for PyTorch). The entire directory is downloaded recursively.

**A** is wrong — models are directories, not single files.

**C** is wrong — the full directory is downloaded, not just the manifest.

**D** is wrong — artifact_path is a relative path within the run's artifact store, which is a valid and required input format.

questions.stem: A model governance policy requires all Production models in the classic registry to have a non-None description AND a tag 'approved_by' set to a reviewer name. A data scientist attempts to promote a version without these. What is the programmatic enforcement pattern?

options.text: Configure a registry webhook that blocks the stage transition if governance checks fail
Before calling transition_model_version_stage(), check version.description is not None and 'approved_by' in client.get_model_version('M', v).tags; raise an error if checks fail
Set a registry-level policy in the Databricks workspace settings that enforces tags before transitions
Use mlflow.validate_model_version() to automatically enforce governance policies

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because programmatic governance gates are implemented as pre-condition checks in code before calling transition_model_version_stage(). The standard pattern is: retrieve the version, check its description and tags, and only proceed if all checks pass.

**A** is wrong because registry webhooks fire asynchronously after events occur and cannot block synchronous stage transitions — they are notification/CI triggers, not access control gates.

**C** is wrong because no workspace-level policy enforcement mechanism exists in MLflow for blocking transitions based on custom field requirements.

**D** is wrong because mlflow.validate_model_version() is not a valid MLflow function — governance validation must be implemented explicitly in application code.

questions.stem: An ML team wants to deploy a new version of their model to Databricks Model Serving with zero downtime. They currently have version 3 serving 100% of traffic. What is the recommended deployment sequence?

options.text: Take the endpoint offline, update it to serve version 4, then bring it back online
Create a new endpoint for version 4 and migrate clients by updating their base URL
Delete version 3 from the registry and register version 4 with the same version number
Configure the endpoint to serve both version 3 and version 4 simultaneously with a traffic split (e.g., 90%/10%), then gradually shift to 100% version 4

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because Databricks Model Serving supports in-place traffic splitting — adding a new model version to an existing endpoint with a portion of traffic allows zero-downtime deployment. The existing version continues serving while the new version is validated.

**A** is wrong because taking the endpoint offline to update it causes downtime during the maintenance window, violating the zero-downtime requirement.

**B** is wrong because creating a new separate endpoint requires all clients to update their base URLs, which adds coordination complexity and is not zero-downtime from the client perspective.

**C** is wrong because version numbers in MLflow are immutable sequential integers — you cannot delete a version and register a new model with the same version number.

[Deploy Models with Model Serving — Databricks Documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/machine-learning/model-serving/index.html)

questions.stem: An ML engineer calls mlflow.deployments.get_deploy_client('databricks') and then uses client.create_endpoint() to deploy a model. What is the primary advantage of using the MLflow Deployments client over configuring the endpoint via the Databricks UI?

options.text: The MLflow Deployments client applies automatic traffic splitting between all registered model versions
Endpoint creation and configuration become code that can be version-controlled, code-reviewed, and automated in CI/CD pipelines
The MLflow Deployments client uses a faster internal API that bypasses Databricks rate limits
The MLflow Deployments client automatically selects the optimal instance type for the model

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because using the MLflow Deployments API (mlflow.deployments.get_deploy_client) to create and configure endpoints makes infrastructure-as-code possible — endpoint configurations live in Python scripts or CI/CD pipelines, can be reviewed, tested, and reproduced across environments. This is the fundamental advantage of API-driven infrastructure over UI-based configuration.

**A** is wrong — automatic traffic splitting is not a feature of the deployments client; traffic config is explicit.

**C** is wrong — the deployments client uses the same Databricks REST API as the UI.

**D** is wrong — instance type selection is explicit in the endpoint configuration.

questions.stem: An ML engineer is deciding whether to use GPU instances for a Databricks Model Serving endpoint. The model is a 100-parameter logistic regression. Which statement best describes when GPU instances are cost-effective for Model Serving?

options.text: GPU instances are always faster than CPU and should be used for any production model

GPU instances are cost-effective for large deep learning models (millions+ of parameters, e.g., transformer-based NLP or image models) where GPU parallelism provides substantial throughput gains over CPU
GPU instances are required for any model trained using PyTorch or TensorFlow
GPU instances should be used when the serving endpoint has more than 100 concurrent requests per second

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because GPU acceleration provides the greatest benefit for large deep learning models with many matrix multiplications that can be parallelized on GPU cores. For a 100-parameter logistic regression, the computation is trivially fast on CPU — GPU overhead (data transfer, kernel launch) would actually add latency at higher cost. GPUs are cost-effective for transformer models, large neural networks, and image models where batch matrix operations saturate GPU compute.

**A** is wrong — for small models, CPU is faster due to lower overhead.

**C** is wrong — PyTorch/TensorFlow models can serve efficiently on CPU if they are small.

**D** is wrong — high concurrency benefits from auto-scaling replicas, not necessarily GPU.

[Deploy Models with Model Serving — Databricks Documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/machine-learning/model-serving/index.html)

questions.stem: An ML engineer wants to perform shadow deployment: the production model (v5) handles all live traffic and returns responses, while a shadow model (v6) receives a copy of each request but its predictions are discarded. What is the purpose of shadow deployment?

options.text: To reduce serving costs by sharing compute between v5 and v6
To automatically switch to v6 if v5 exceeds its latency SLA
To allow v6 to learn from v5's predictions via online learning
To test v6 under real production traffic patterns — including edge cases and load levels — without any risk of affecting production outputs

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because shadow deployment (also called shadow mode or dark launch) is specifically designed to evaluate a new model version against real production traffic without affecting production responses. v6 receives the same input as v5, generates predictions, and those predictions are logged for offline analysis. This allows the team to compare v6's behavior against real-world data distributions and edge cases that may not be present in test sets.

**A** is wrong — shadow deployment actually increases cost because both models process each request.

**C** is wrong — shadow deployment does not involve online learning.

**B** is wrong — shadow deployment does not implement automatic failover.

[Deploy Models with Model Serving — Databricks Documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/machine-learning/model-serving/index.html)

questions.stem: An ML engineer is implementing a pyfunc PythonModel that wraps a text preprocessing step (tokenization) and a BERT classification model. Select TWO best practices for the predict() method implementation. (Select TWO)

options.text: Load the BERT model and tokenizer in predict() on each call to ensure the latest weights are used
Load the BERT model and tokenizer in load_context() once at model load time, storing them as instance attributes
Accept both pandas DataFrame and numpy array inputs in predict() by checking isinstance(model_input, pd.DataFrame)
Return predictions as a plain Python list to maximize compatibility with downstream consumers

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because load_context() is called once when the model is loaded for serving, making it the correct place to initialize expensive objects like BERT models and tokenizers. Storing them as self.bert_model and self.tokenizer avoids re-initialization on every request.

**A** is wrong because loading BERT in predict() on every call would add seconds of latency per request and repeatedly allocate GPU memory.

**C** is correct because production pyfunc models receive inputs in different formats depending on the calling context (pandas from Spark UDF, dict from REST endpoint) — defensive type checking enables the model to handle both.

**D** is wrong — returning a pandas Series or DataFrame is preferred for type safety, indexing, and downstream compatibility with spark_udf result_type expectations.

[mlflow.pyfunc — MLflow Python API](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/python_api/mlflow.pyfunc.html)

questions.stem: A Databricks Model Serving endpoint receives 10,000 requests per minute at peak. The endpoint is configured with provisioned concurrency of 10. Each request takes ~50ms. How many requests will queue (not be immediately served) at peak load?

options.text: None — the endpoint auto-scales instantly to handle any load
Approximately 10,000 * 0.050/60 = 8.3 concurrent requests needed; with concurrency 10, all requests are served with headroom
All 10,000 requests queue because provisioned concurrency only handles one request at a time
10,000 - 10 = 9,990 requests queue because only 10 can be served simultaneously

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. At 10,000 requests per minute with 50ms per request, the required concurrency is (10,000 req/min) × (50ms/req) × (1 min/60,000ms) ≈ 8.3 concurrent requests. With provisioned concurrency of 10, the endpoint has sufficient capacity (10 > 8.3) to handle peak load without queuing, leaving ~1.7 slots of headroom.

**A** is wrong — auto-scaling is not instant; provisioned concurrency is what handles immediate load.

**C** is wrong — provisioned concurrency of 10 means 10 simultaneous requests, not one at a time.

**D** is wrong — the queue depth is not simply total_requests minus concurrency; it depends on arrival rate vs. service rate.

questions.stem: A data science team builds a custom monitoring solution using Evidently AI to generate data drift reports. Compared to Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring, what is the primary tradeoff of this approach?

options.text: Evidently AI reports are less accurate than Lakehouse Monitoring statistical tests
Custom Evidently AI monitoring requires the team to build and maintain orchestration, storage, and alerting infrastructure that Lakehouse Monitoring provides out-of-the-box
Evidently AI does not support numerical feature drift detection
Evidently AI reports cannot be scheduled and must be run manually

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the primary tradeoff of custom monitoring frameworks like Evidently AI vs. managed solutions like Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring is the infrastructure burden. Evidently AI provides rich drift reports and visualizations, but the team must integrate it with a job scheduler, a data store for drift metrics, a dashboard tool, and an alerting system. Lakehouse Monitoring provides a fully managed end-to-end pipeline with Delta table outputs, scheduled refresh, and built-in integration with Databricks SQL dashboards.

**A** is wrong — Evidently AI's statistical tests are rigorous and often more customizable.

**C** is wrong — Evidently AI supports numerical, categorical, and text drift.

**D** is wrong — Evidently AI can be scheduled via any orchestrator.

[Machine Learning on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/machine-learning/index.html)

questions.stem: A monitoring team runs a KS test on a revenue feature: from scipy.stats import ks_2samp; stat, p = ks_2samp(train_values, prod_values). The result is stat=0.04, p=0.62. How should this result be interpreted?

options.text: The large KS statistic of 0.04 relative to the p-value indicates significant drift
The p-value of 0.62 is well above the 0.05 significance threshold, meaning we fail to reject the null hypothesis — there is no statistically significant evidence of drift
A p-value of 0.62 means 62% of the distribution has drifted
The KS statistic of 0.04 confirms exactly 4% drift in the feature values

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because a p-value of 0.62 means that under the null hypothesis (the two samples come from the same distribution), there is a 62% probability of observing a KS statistic as large as 0.04 by chance alone. Since 0.62 >> 0.05 (the common significance level), we fail to reject the null hypothesis — the evidence does not support concluding there is distributional drift.

**A** is wrong — 0.04 is a small KS statistic (max possible is 1.0); the interpretation must use the p-value.

**C** is wrong — p-values are not percentages of drift.

**D** is wrong — the KS statistic measures the maximum absolute difference between CDFs, not a percentage of feature values that drifted.
