questions.stem: A production recommendation model has a 14-day ground truth latency (user purchases are recorded with a 2-week delay). The team wants to monitor model quality in near-real-time. Which approach best addresses this latency constraint?

options.text: Wait 14 days before computing any monitoring metrics — accuracy cannot be estimated without true labels
Use prediction distribution monitoring as a leading indicator of model health, and compute true accuracy metrics on a 14-day rolling lag when labels become available
Retrain the model every 14 days regardless of drift to ensure it is always current
Switch to a simpler rule-based model that does not require monitoring

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the standard approach for ground truth latency uses a two-layer strategy: leading indicators available immediately (prediction score drift, input feature drift, proxy metrics) plus lagged true quality metrics computed on the rolling labeled window.

**A** is wrong because waiting 14 days before any monitoring means silent degradation goes undetected for two weeks — proxy monitoring during the label latency window is valuable even without exact accuracy numbers.

**C** is wrong because unconditional retraining every 14 days wastes compute when the model is performing well — retraining should be triggered by detected degradation, not a fixed calendar.

**D** is wrong because replacing an ML model with rule-based logic to avoid monitoring complexity defeats the purpose — it eliminates the ML capability without actually solving the monitoring problem.

[Machine Learning on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/machine-learning/index.html)

questions.stem: A team observes that their classification model's predicted positive rate has increased from 5% at training time to 22% in production. The input feature distributions appear stable. What type of drift is this, and what is the most likely explanation?

options.text: Data drift — the input features have shifted even though the KS test shows no significant change

Prediction drift (output drift) — the model's predicted label distribution has shifted, potentially indicating the true positive rate in the population has genuinely increased, or concept drift has caused the model to misclassify
Model version drift — a different model version was accidentally deployed
Label drift — the training labels were imbalanced, causing the model to over-predict positives

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because when input feature distributions are stable but the predicted label distribution shifts (5% → 22% positive rate), this is prediction drift or output drift. The cause may be genuine population change or concept drift.

**A** is wrong because data drift refers to P(X) change — shifts in the input feature distribution. The scenario explicitly states that input feature distributions appear stable, ruling out data drift.

**C** is wrong because model version drift would typically cause sudden discontinuous changes at a deployment event, not a gradual shift from 5% to 22% positive rate over time.

**D** is wrong because label drift refers to changes in the training label distribution (a historical artifact), not the distribution of predictions in production.

[Machine Learning on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/machine-learning/index.html)

questions.stem: A team uses Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring on an inference table with a TIMESERIES monitor. They notice the daily accuracy metric in the drift metrics table is NULL for several days. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: Accuracy cannot be computed for TIMESERIES monitors — use InferenceLog type instead
The ground truth label column contains NULL values for those days, making accuracy computation impossible
The monitoring job failed on those days and the metrics were not written
The inference table had fewer rows than the minimum threshold for those time windows, causing Lakehouse Monitoring to suppress metrics to avoid statistically unreliable results

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring applies a minimum row count threshold per time window before computing aggregate statistics and model quality metrics. If a particular daily window contains too few rows (e.g., during a low-traffic weekend or a data pipeline outage that delayed writes), the monitor suppresses metrics for that window rather than reporting unreliable statistics computed from very small samples.

**A** is wrong — TIMESERIES monitors do compute accuracy if labels are available.

**B** is partially plausible but NULLs in labels would typically result in metric computation on non-null rows, not complete suppression.

**C** is possible but would affect all metrics, not just accuracy for specific windows.

[Machine Learning on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/machine-learning/index.html)

questions.stem: An ML platform team is designing monitoring for a recommendation system. They cannot directly observe ground truth (whether users would have clicked a recommendation they didn't see). Select TWO proxy monitoring approaches appropriate for this case. (Select TWO)

options.text: Track the click-through rate (CTR) on recommended items as a business proxy metric for recommendation quality
Compute precision@k directly against future purchases as the ground truth, ignoring the causality problem
Monitor the distribution of recommendation score outputs for drift — a significant shift in score distribution without input drift may indicate model degradation
Replace the ML model with a rule-based system to eliminate the ground truth latency problem

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because CTR on recommended items is a real-time, observable business proxy metric that correlates with recommendation quality. A sustained decline in CTR is an actionable signal of model degradation even without explicit relevance labels.

**B** is wrong because computing precision@k against future purchases suffers from severe selection bias — users can only purchase items they were shown, so this metric is confounded by the model's own recommendations and cannot serve as unbiased ground truth.

**C** is correct because monitoring recommendation score distributions provides a statistical proxy — if the model starts assigning systematically different scores to similar item-user pairs (e.g., all scores collapsing near 0.5), this signals potential model issues without requiring ground truth labels.

**D** is wrong because replacing the ML model with a rule-based system to avoid the ground truth problem abandons ML monitoring rather than solving it — it does not address the underlying challenge.
